macro_segments.title: Wine that is souring: Does it retain the status of wine for blessings and terumah?

micro_segments.title: Introduction: The quality of store-bought wine (kosa deshotah) and the question of the appropriate blessing
Rav Yosef's source: A baraita about checking a barrel before designating terumah from it
The timeline problem: When was the wine definitely good, when definitely vinegar, when doubtful?

macro_segments.title: Blessings on grape-derived drinks and non-grape beverages

micro_segments.title: Tosafot note on testing: How did one taste the barrel when it was still tevel (untithed)?

macro_segments.title: Wine that is souring: Does it retain the status of wine for blessings and terumah? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's position: First three days definitely wine; souring process still counts as wine
Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi's position: Last three days definitely vinegar; souring counts as vinegar
Resolution: The position depends on whether strong vinegar was found; souring is treated as wine

macro_segments.title: Sale of wine that became vinegar: Is it grounds for returning merchandise (mekach taut)?

micro_segments.title: The case: Buyer purchases wine, tastes it in store as good, finds it vinegar days later at home
Rav's position: Within three days it was definitely already souring when sold—mekach taut applies
Shmuel's position: Wine carries the mazal of its owner; buyer cannot claim mekach taut
Tosafot alternative: Transport by the buyer unsettled the wine and caused early spoilage
Rav Yosef's practical ruling: Accepted Rav's position for beer, Shmuel's for wine
Final halacha: We rule like Shmuel that wine cannot be returned even within three days

macro_segments.title: Blessings on grape-derived drinks and non-grape beverages (continued)

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Mead from dates, beer from barley, and grape-flavored water all require shehakol blessing
Principle: Non-wine fruit juices are considered inferior and do not retain their original blessing
Explanation: Wine and oil are exceptions; other juices (dates, barley) lose their blessings when processed

macro_segments.title: Water infused with grape skins: At what ratio does it become wine again?

micro_segments.title: Setup: Adding water to grape skins (lees) extracts grapey flavor; determining the proportion of actual grape juice
Consensus case 2: Three units in, four units out—at least one unit is grape juice, counts as wine
Consensus case 1: Three units in, three units out—no significant grape juice, water only
Dispute: Three units in, three-and-a-half out. Are there one-half units or one full unit of grape juice extracted?
Majority view: Assume only one-half unit of grape juice (1:6 ratio), not wine; minority view assumes one full unit (1:2.5 ratio)
Connection to Mishnah: Debate mirrors disagreement in ma'aser (tithing) about when water-soaked grape must be tithed
Tosafot: Modern grape-skin wine practice does not meet the 1:3 ratio requirement; we rule shehakol

macro_segments.title: Rav's wine-to-water mixing ratio and the Sanhedrin quorum rule (al yechsar hamazeg)

micro_segments.title: Rav's principle: Wine diluted with three parts water is the standard mixture (1:3 ratio)
Kiddush cup requirement: Must start with one-quarter log of wine to end with one-quarter log after dilution
Tosafot excursus: The Mishnaic phrase 'al yechsar hamazeg' applied to Sanhedrin quorum rules
Tosafot gematria interpretation: Mazeg (50 by gematria) means 50 members cannot leave the 71-member Sanhedrin

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Yochanan vs Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi
Rav vs Shmuel on wine sales
fruit juice status (not wine or oil)
